walkthrough.title: Lay Out the Time Board
walkthrough.text: Let's get Patchwork set up. First, place the central time board in the middle of the table. It has two sides with different artwork, but they work exactly the same way. Pick whichever side you like better.

walkthrough.title: Place the Time Tokens
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one time token. One is lime green and the other is yellow. Place both time tokens on the starting space of the time board. These tokens will track your progress through the game.

walkthrough.title: Place the Special Patches
walkthrough.text: Find the five small leather patches. These are single-square pieces. Place them on the five marked spaces of the time board. You will pick these up as you pass over those spaces during the game.

walkthrough.title: Set Out the Special Tile
walkthrough.text: Find the special tile worth seven bonus points. Place it near the time board where both players can see it. The first player to completely fill a seven-by-seven area on their quilt board will claim this tile.

walkthrough.title: Arrange the Patch Market
walkthrough.text: Now take all thirty-three regular patches and lay them face up in a circle or oval around the time board. These form the patch market that both players will buy from throughout the game. Make sure every patch is visible.

walkthrough.title: Place the Neutral Token
walkthrough.text: Find the smallest patch in the circle. This is the one-by-two patch. Place the neutral token between that patch and the next patch going clockwise. The neutral token marks where the available patches start. On each turn, you can only choose from the three patches clockwise in front of this token.

walkthrough.title: Set Up the Button Supply
walkthrough.text: Place all the button tiles in a shared supply near the time board. There are tiles worth one, five, ten, and twenty buttons. Both players will pay into and collect from this supply throughout the game.

walkthrough.title: Player Setup and Starting the Game
walkthrough.text: Each player takes one quilt board and five buttons from the supply. Your quilt board is a nine-by-nine grid where you will place your patches. The player who last used a needle goes first. Remember, turns do not alternate. The player whose time token is further behind on the time board always goes next. If both tokens are on the same space, the one on top goes next.

summary.title: Player Setup and Starting the Game
bullets: Each player takes 1 quilt board (9x9 grid) and 5 buttons
Player who last used a needle goes first
Turns don't alternate — furthest-behind token always goes next
Same space → token on top goes next

walkthrough.title: Who Goes Next
walkthrough.text: Patchwork does not use alternating turns. Instead, the player whose time token is further behind on the time board always takes the next turn. This means one player can take several turns in a row if their token stays behind. If both tokens are on the same space, the token on top goes next.

walkthrough.title: Your Two Action Choices
walkthrough.text: On your turn, you must take exactly one of two actions. Action A is Advance and Receive Buttons. Action B is Take and Place a Patch. You cannot do both. Let's walk through each one.

walkthrough.title: Action A: Advance and Receive Buttons
walkthrough.text: This is the simpler action. Move your time token forward to the space directly in front of your opponent's token. You receive one button from the supply for each space you moved. This is a great way to earn quick income when you cannot afford a patch or when nothing good is available.

walkthrough.title: Action B: Take and Place a Patch
walkthrough.text: This is the core action of the game, and it has five steps that must happen in order. First, choose one of the three patches directly clockwise in front of the neutral token. Second, move the neutral token to sit next to the chosen patch. Third, pay the patch's button cost to the supply. Fourth, place the patch on your quilt board. Fifth, move your time token forward by the patch's time cost.

walkthrough.title: Placing Patches on Your Quilt Board
walkthrough.text: When you place a patch on your quilt board, you can rotate it and flip it any way you want. The only rule is that it cannot overlap any patch already on your board. Every empty square at the end of the game costs you two points, so try to fill your board as completely as possible.

walkthrough.title: Time Board Events: Button Income
walkthrough.text: Count every button icon printed on the patches already placed on your quilt board. The supply pays you that many buttons. Patches with more button icons generate more income each time you pass these spaces.

walkthrough.title: Time Board Events: Special Patches
walkthrough.text: These are single-square pieces and they are the only way to fill individual empty squares. Once taken, they are gone, so the first player to reach each one gets it.

walkthrough.title: The Special Tile
walkthrough.text: The first player to completely fill any seven-by-seven area on their quilt board claims the special tile. It is worth seven bonus points at the end of the game. Once claimed, the other player cannot earn it. This is a significant bonus, but chasing it at the expense of leaving big gaps elsewhere can backfire.

walkthrough.title: Understanding the Patch Labels
walkthrough.text: Each patch has two numbers on its label. The button cost tells you how many buttons to pay from your supply. The time cost tells you how many spaces to move your time token forward. Some patches also show button-income icons, which means they will earn you buttons every time you pass an income space on the time board.

walkthrough.title: End of the Game and Scoring
walkthrough.text: The game ends when both time tokens reach the last space of the time board. If your token would move past the last space, it simply stops there. Your final score equals the buttons you have remaining, plus seven if you have the special tile, minus two for each empty square on your quilt board. The highest score wins. If tied, the player who reached the final space first wins.

summary.title: Who Goes Next
bullets: Furthest-behind time token always takes the next turn
One player can take multiple turns in a row
Same space → token on top goes next

walkthrough.title: Welcome to Your First Game
walkthrough.text: In this practice tutorial, we will walk through a scripted opening between two players. We will call them Player A and Player B. Player A goes first because they last used a needle. Both players start with five buttons each and empty quilt boards. Let's go.

walkthrough.title: Scanning the Market
walkthrough.text: Before taking your first turn, look at the three patches available clockwise in front of the neutral token. Each patch shows a button cost, a time cost, and possibly button-income icons. In the early game, you want patches that give you button income so you can afford better patches later. Think of button-income patches as investments that keep paying you back.

walkthrough.title: Player A's First Turn: Grab an Income Patch
walkthrough.text: Player A spots a patch among the three available that has two button-income icons. It costs three buttons and has a time cost of two. Player A pays three buttons to the supply, leaving them with two buttons. They place the patch on their quilt board near the center and advance their time token two spaces. Smart pick. Every time Player A passes an income space, they will earn two extra buttons.

walkthrough.title: Why Income Early Matters
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about why that was a great opening. The time board has several button-income spaces scattered along it. Each time your token passes one, you collect buttons for every income icon on your quilt board. Getting income patches early means you collect from them multiple times throughout the game. Waiting until late game to buy income patches means fewer payouts and weaker buying power overall.

walkthrough.title: Player B's First Turn: A Common Mistake
walkthrough.text: Player B sees a big five-square patch among the available three. It costs seven buttons. Player B only has five buttons, so they cannot afford it. Instead of looking at the other two available patches, Player B decides to take Action A and advance past Player A's token to earn buttons. Player B moves forward and earns three buttons, bringing their total to eight.

walkthrough.title: Why That Was Wasteful
walkthrough.text: Player B burned a lot of time on the track just to afford one expensive patch. Meanwhile, Player A stayed behind and will likely get multiple turns before Player B goes again. Advancing too far for a single patch eats into your total game time and gives your opponent free turns. It is almost always better to buy a cheaper patch you can afford right now than to skip ahead just to save up for an expensive one.

walkthrough.title: Player A's Consecutive Turns
walkthrough.text: Because Player A's token is still behind Player B's, Player A gets to go again. Player A scans the three available patches and finds a small two-square patch with one button-income icon. It costs two buttons and has a time cost of one. Player A buys it, places it on their quilt board, and advances one space. Player A is still behind Player B, so they get yet another turn.

walkthrough.title: The Power of Staying Behind
walkthrough.text: This is one of the most important concepts in Patchwork. Low-time patches let you stay behind your opponent on the time board, which means you keep taking turns. Player A just took two consecutive turns while Player B sat waiting. Meanwhile, Player A now has three button-income icons on their board, building a strong economy for the rest of the game.

walkthrough.title: Player B's Mistake: The Big Patch
walkthrough.text: Player B finally takes their turn and buys that big expensive patch. It costs seven buttons and has a time cost of five. Player B pays seven buttons, places the large piece in the corner of their board, and moves forward five spaces. The patch looks impressive on the board, but it has zero button-income icons and it pushed Player B far ahead on the time track.

walkthrough.title: Why Big Early Patches Backfire
walkthrough.text: Big patches without income icons are a trap in the early game. They eat your buttons and your time, which are the two resources that matter most. Player B spent all their buttons on a patch that will never generate income. Now they have one button left, they are far ahead on the time track, and Player A gets even more consecutive turns. Save the big space-fillers for later when you have income flowing.

walkthrough.title: Player A Passes an Income Space
walkthrough.text: Player A buys another small patch and advances their token past the first button-income space on the time board. Income triggers. Player A counts the button-income icons on their quilt board: three icons total. The supply pays Player A three buttons. This is pure profit that Player A will collect again and again at each income space.

walkthrough.title: Player B's Mistake: Leaving Gaps
walkthrough.text: A few turns later, Player B has placed three patches on their board but left several isolated single-square gaps between them. These gaps cannot be filled by regular patches because no regular patch is smaller than one-by-two. The only way to fill single-square holes is with the leather patches from the time board, and there are only five of those in the entire game.

walkthrough.title: Lesson: Place Patches Tightly
walkthrough.text: Every empty square on your quilt board costs you two points at the end of the game. Single-square gaps are especially dangerous because regular patches cannot fill them. When placing a patch, always think about what shapes could fill the remaining spaces around it. Start from a corner or edge and build outward to minimize awkward gaps. A little planning now saves a lot of penalty points later.

walkthrough.title: Player A Collects a Leather Patch
walkthrough.text: Player A's token reaches one of the five leather-patch spaces on the time board. Player A takes the leather patch immediately and places it on their quilt board, filling a single-square gap that was starting to form. Perfect. These leather patches are precious, so plan your placement to use them wisely.

walkthrough.title: Mid-Game Check-In
walkthrough.text: Let's compare the two players. Player A has five patches on their board with three button-income icons, no isolated gaps, and a healthy button supply. Player B has three patches, zero income icons, several gaps forming, and almost no buttons. The difference came down to three decisions: Player A prioritized income, bought small efficient patches, and placed them carefully.

walkthrough.title: Your Turn to Play
walkthrough.text: You have seen the key patterns now. Prioritize button-income patches in the early game. Buy patches you can afford rather than skipping ahead to save up. Stay behind on the time board to earn consecutive turns. Place patches tightly to avoid single-square gaps. Continue playing from here and put these lessons into practice.

walkthrough.title: Before You Stitch
walkthrough.text:
Before you dive into a full game, here are three things that can help.

First, check out General Tips for strategy basics on patch evaluation and time management.

Second, reference the Appendix for a quick guide to patch types, board events, and scoring.

And most importantly, ask your personal GM Guide for help anytime. Just tap the Q&A tab and ask any question during your game. Have fun!

walkthrough.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
walkthrough.text: Before we talk strategy, let's cover the biggest beginner traps. First, buying big patches with zero income early in the game drains your buttons and time without paying you back. Second, ignoring button-income patches leaves you broke in the mid-game when the best patches come around. Third, creating isolated single-square gaps on your quilt board that only leather patches can fill. Fourth, advancing too far on the time track just to afford one patch when cheaper options are available right now. Keep these in mind and you will avoid the most common pitfalls.

walkthrough.title: Evaluating Patches
walkthrough.text: Every patch has three things to consider. The button cost tells you what you pay now. The time cost tells you how far your token advances on the time track. And the button-income icons tell you how much the patch will earn you every time you pass an income space. A cheap patch with income icons is often more valuable than an expensive flashy patch with none. Also consider the shape. A patch that fits cleanly against your existing pieces is worth more than one that creates awkward gaps, even if the numbers look better.

walkthrough.title: The Time Track is Your Clock
walkthrough.text: Think of the time track as a budget you spend throughout the game. Every patch you buy costs time, and when your token reaches the end, your game is over. Buying a patch with a time cost of six is like spending six turns at once. Low-time patches let you stay behind your opponent and take more total turns. More turns means more patches, more income collections, and a fuller board. Manage your time budget carefully because you cannot get it back.

walkthrough.title: When to Advance for Buttons
walkthrough.text: Action A, advancing to collect buttons, is not just a fallback option. It is a real strategic tool. Use it when all three available patches are bad shapes or overpriced for what they offer. Use it when you are one or two buttons short of a great patch that is about to rotate into your three-patch window. And use it when you are ahead on the time board and need to let your opponent catch up before you can take another turn anyway. Just be careful not to advance too far or you will give up valuable turns.

walkthrough.title: Build From a Corner
walkthrough.text: Start placing patches from one corner of your quilt board and work outward. This creates a solid filled area with a clean boundary instead of scattered patches with holes everywhere. As you build outward, always look at the boundary edge and think about what patch shapes would extend it smoothly. The goal is to maintain a frontier with as few isolated single-square holes as possible. Remember, only the five leather patches from the time board can fill single-square gaps.

walkthrough.title: The Seven-by-Seven Bonus
walkthrough.text: The special tile is worth seven points, which is a massive swing in a game where final scores are often in the teens or twenties. But do not chase it blindly. If filling a seven-by-seven area forces you to leave big gaps in the rest of your board, the penalty points from those gaps can outweigh the seven-point bonus. Pursue the special tile when your patch shapes naturally support a dense seven-by-seven cluster, not when it requires sacrificing board efficiency.

walkthrough.title: Reading Your Opponent
walkthrough.text: Patchwork is a two-player game, so always pay attention to what your opponent needs. If they are eyeing a specific patch in the market, consider whether buying a patch in front of it will move the neutral token past their target. You can also use the advance action strategically to land your token just ahead of theirs, forcing them to make the next move. Watching the market and your opponent's board can reveal which patches they desperately need and which ones you can deny them.

walkthrough.title: Late-Game Priorities
walkthrough.text: In the last third of the game, your priorities shift. Income patches are no longer valuable because there are fewer income spaces left to pass. Instead, focus on patches that fill the most empty squares on your board for the lowest time cost. Every square you fill saves you two penalty points. This is also when the big space-filling patches shine because the game is ending anyway and you need coverage more than income. Count your empty squares and do the math: filling three squares saves you six points, which is almost as good as the special tile.

summary.title: Common Mistakes to Avoid
bullets: Don't buy big patches with 0 income early — drains buttons and time
Don't ignore income patches — you'll be broke mid-game
Don't create isolated 1x1 gaps — only leather patches can fill them
Don't advance far on the track just to afford 1 expensive patch

Shape matters — clean fit beats better numbers with awkward gaps

walkthrough.title: Tempo Control
walkthrough.text: The most powerful concept in Patchwork is tempo control. By choosing low-time patches, you stay behind your opponent on the time board and earn consecutive turns. A chain of three small patches bought in a row can cover more total squares than one large patch while also giving you three income collections instead of one. Think of each turn as currency. The player who takes more total turns over the course of the game has a massive structural advantage.

walkthrough.title: Income Engine Math
walkthrough.text: Let's talk about exact numbers. The time board has multiple button-income spaces spread across it. If you pick up a patch with two income icons early and pass five income spaces afterward, that one patch earns you ten buttons over the course of the game. Compare that to a patch with zero income icons that saves you a few empty-square penalties. Do the math before each purchase: multiply the income icons by the approximate number of income spaces remaining. If the total exceeds the patch's cost, it is paying for itself.

walkthrough.title: Patch Shape Planning
walkthrough.text: Advanced players plan their quilt board several patches ahead. Before buying a patch, mentally place it on your board and ask: what shapes will I need to fill the spaces around it? If the answer is shapes that commonly appear in the market, go for it. If the answer is shapes that are rare or already bought, reconsider. Building from a corner with consistent geometry creates a predictable frontier that many patch shapes can extend. Avoid creating nooks that require very specific shapes to fill.

walkthrough.title: Market Manipulation
walkthrough.text: When you buy a patch, the neutral token moves to its location. This changes which three patches become available for your opponent. Before buying, look past the immediate purchase and consider what you are revealing. Sometimes buying the second or third patch in the window is better than the first because it skips the neutral token further ahead and hides a strong patch from your opponent. Conversely, sometimes advancing for buttons is better than buying, because it leaves the neutral token in place and denies your opponent a market refresh.

walkthrough.title: The Leather Patch Race
walkthrough.text: There are only five leather patches on the time board, and they are the only way to fill single-square gaps. Advanced players plan their time token movement to land on or pass leather-patch spaces before their opponent. If you can see that your next patch purchase will advance you past a leather patch while your opponent is still behind it, that is a double win. You fill a gap on your board and deny your opponent the same resource. Always check the time board for upcoming leather patches before choosing your action.

walkthrough.title: When to Sacrifice Tempo for Value
walkthrough.text: Staying behind is generally correct, but sometimes the right play is a big leap forward. If a high-time patch has three income icons and fills a difficult section of your board perfectly, the value may outweigh the lost turns. The test is simple: will the income generated plus the penalty points saved exceed what you could earn from the consecutive small turns you are giving up? When the answer is yes, take the big patch. This usually happens in the early-to-mid game when many income spaces remain.

walkthrough.title: Endgame Board Optimization
walkthrough.text: In the final stretch, count your empty squares and calculate the maximum you can fill with remaining turns. If you have eight empty squares and can realistically buy two more patches, target the shapes that fill the most of those specific gaps. At this point, cost and income are irrelevant. The only thing that matters is coverage per time. A free one-by-two patch that fills two critical gaps is worth more than an expensive four-square patch that overlaps with areas you have already filled. Shift entirely to spatial optimization and ignore economy.

walkthrough.title: Seven-by-Seven Racing Strategy
walkthrough.text: The special tile is worth seven points, but the real question is: how many points does your opponent lose if you take it? The answer is fourteen, because they lose the seven points they could have gained and you gain seven. That fourteen-point swing makes the special tile the single highest-impact element in the game. If you and your opponent are both close to completing a seven-by-seven area, prioritize it aggressively. If only you are close, pursue it at moderate priority. If neither of you is close, ignore it and focus on board coverage and income.

summary.title: Tempo Control
bullets: Low-time patches → stay behind → more consecutive turns
3 small patches often > 1 big patch in total coverage
More turns = more income collections, more patches, fuller board
Each turn is currency — the player with more turns has structural advantage

summary.title: Income Engine Math
bullets: Income icons × remaining income spaces = total payout
2 income icons early can earn 10+ buttons over the game
If total payout > patch cost, the patch pays for itself
Do this math before every purchase

summary.title: Patch Shape Planning
bullets: Plan 2-3 patches ahead on your quilt board
Ask: what shapes fill the gaps this creates? Are those shapes available?
Build from corner with consistent geometry
Avoid nooks requiring very specific shapes

summary.title: Market Manipulation
bullets: Buying patch 2 or 3 skips the neutral token further → hides patches from opponent
Advancing for buttons leaves neutral token in place → no market refresh
Consider what 3 patches you reveal to opponent after your buy
Deny strong patches when possible

summary.title: The Leather Patch Race
bullets: Only 5 leather patches exist — plan time movement to collect them
Reaching a leather patch first = fill your gap + deny opponent
Check time board for upcoming leather patches before every action
Leather patches are the only way to fill 1x1 holes

summary.title: When to Sacrifice Tempo for Value
bullets: Big leap OK if: income payout + penalty savings > lost consecutive turns
Usually correct in early-mid game with many income spaces remaining
High-income patches (3 icons) + perfect fit = worth the time cost
Test: will this patch earn back more than the turns I give up?

summary.title: Endgame Board Optimization
bullets: Count empty squares, calculate max fillable with remaining turns
Target shapes that fill your specific gaps, ignore cost/income
Coverage per time is the only metric that matters late
Small patches filling critical gaps > big patches with overlap

summary.title: Seven-by-Seven Racing Strategy
bullets: Real value = 14-point swing (you gain 7 + opponent loses 7)
Both close to 7x7 → prioritize aggressively
Only you close → pursue at moderate priority
Neither close → ignore it, focus on coverage and income

entries.definition: The central board where both time tokens travel. Contains button-income spaces and special-patch spaces. Both sides are cosmetically different but functionally identical. The game ends when both tokens reach the final space.
Each player's marker on the time board. The player whose token is further behind always takes the next turn. If both are on the same space, the one on top goes next. Lime green and yellow.

Placed in the patch market circle. Marks the starting point for the three available patches. When a patch is bought, the neutral token moves to sit next to the purchased patch. Does not move when a player chooses Action A.
Each player's personal 9x9 grid where patches are placed. Empty squares at game end cost 2 penalty points each. The goal is to fill as much of the board as possible.
The game's currency and victory points. Used to buy patches during the game and counted as points at game end. Come in denominations of 1, 5, 10, and 20. Stored in a shared supply.

The 33 fabric patches arranged in a circle around the time board. Each has a button cost, time cost, and shape. Some also have button-income icons. Available for purchase three at a time from in front of the neutral token.
Five single-square patches placed on marked spaces of the time board. Collected by the first player to move onto or past each space. The only way to fill individual 1x1 gaps on the quilt board.

Worth 7 bonus points. Claimed by the first player to completely fill any 7x7 area on their quilt board. Once claimed, it is unavailable to the other player. A major scoring swing worth pursuing when natural placement supports it.

entries.term: Action A: Advance and Receive Buttons
entries.definition: Move your time token to the space directly in front of your opponent's token. Receive 1 button from the supply for each space moved. If movement would pass the final space, stop on the final space and only receive buttons for actual spaces moved.

entries.term: Action B: Take and Place a Patch
entries.definition: Five steps in order: choose one of three available patches, move neutral token next to it, pay button cost to supply, place patch on quilt board without overlap, advance time token by the patch's time cost.
The number of buttons a player must pay to the supply when purchasing a patch. Printed on the patch label. Must have enough buttons to afford the patch before selecting it.

The number of spaces a player's time token advances when purchasing a patch. Printed on the patch label. Higher time costs push you further ahead on the time board, potentially giving your opponent consecutive turns.

Small button symbols printed on some patches. Each icon generates 1 button of income every time you pass a button-income space on the time board. Patches with more icons are more valuable early in the game when many income spaces remain.

Marked spaces on the time board that trigger income collection. When your time token moves onto or past one, count all button-income icons on your quilt board and receive that many buttons from the supply.

Five marked spaces on the time board with leather patches. When your time token moves onto or past one, take the leather patch immediately and place it on your quilt board. First player to reach each space gets its patch.

When one player's time token remains behind the other after taking a turn, that player takes another turn immediately. This can happen multiple times in a row with low-time patches. A core strategic concept in Patchwork.
The three patches directly clockwise in front of the neutral token. Only these three can be purchased on any given turn. The window shifts each time a patch is bought because the neutral token moves.
At game end, each uncovered square on your quilt board costs 2 points, subtracted from your button total. On a 9x9 board (81 squares), unfilled spaces can result in devastating penalties.
Buttons remaining + special tile bonus (7 if claimed) - empty-space penalties (2 per uncovered square). Highest score wins. Tie-break goes to the player who reached the final time-board space first.
The first player to completely fill any contiguous 7x7 area on their quilt board claims the special tile worth 7 points. This is a race — once one player claims it, the other cannot earn it.
